salary.title: Salary
salary.description: A small classic dataset with 30 records mapping years of professional experience to annual salary. Ideal for understanding single-variable linear regression.

graduate-admissions.title: Graduate Admissions
graduate-admissions.description: 400 records of graduate school applicants with GRE/TOEFL scores, university rating, SOP/LOR strength, CGPA and research experience, used to predict the chance of admission.

uber-fares.title: Uber Fares
uber-fares.description: A sample of Uber trips in New York City with pickup/dropoff coordinates and passenger count, used to predict the fare amount in USD.

common.tagline: Learn Machine Learning by doing. Train models, make predictions, and understand algorithms — all in your browser.

actions.trainFirst: Train a model first to make predictions.

linear-regression.name: Linear Regression
linear-regression.summary: Predict numeric values from continuous data
linear-regression.contextDescription: Real estate companies use linear regression to estimate rental prices based on area, number of rooms, and location. By identifying the relationship between property features and prices, they can provide accurate valuations for new listings.
linear-regression.contextExtra1: Salary prediction based on years of experience
linear-regression.contextExtra2: Sales forecasting based on advertising spend
linear-regression.contextExtra5: Crop yield prediction based on weather data
linear-regression.contextExtra6: Insurance premium estimation from risk factors

logistic-regression.name: Logistic Regression
logistic-regression.contextDescription: Telecom companies use logistic regression to predict which customers are likely to cancel their subscriptions. By analyzing usage patterns, contract type, and support interactions, they can proactively retain at-risk customers.

decision-tree.name: Decision Tree
decision-tree.summary: Make decisions through a series of questions
decision-tree.contextDescription: Hospitals use decision trees to assist in patient triage. By evaluating symptoms, age, and test results in a structured sequence of decisions, they can quickly prioritize patients and suggest preliminary diagnoses.

knn.name: KNN
knn.summary: Classify by finding similar examples nearby
knn.contextDescription: Streaming platforms use K-Nearest Neighbors to recommend movies. By finding movies similar to ones you've enjoyed — based on genre, duration, and ratings — the algorithm suggests titles you're likely to watch next.
knn.contextExtra6: Real-time pricing based on competitor data

svm.name: SVM
svm.summary: Find the optimal boundary between classes
svm.contextDescription: Banks use Support Vector Machines to detect fraudulent transactions. By analyzing transaction amount, time, and location patterns, SVM finds the optimal boundary separating legitimate transactions from suspicious ones.
svm.contextExtra4: Spam image detection in social media
svm.contextExtra6: Cancer cell detection in medical scans

random-forest.name: Random Forest
random-forest.summary: Combine many decision trees for better accuracy
random-forest.contextDescription: Financial institutions use Random Forest to evaluate credit applications. By combining hundreds of decision trees that each analyze income, credit history, and debt ratios, the ensemble model provides robust and accurate approval decisions.
random-forest.contextExtra2: Disease risk assessment from patient records
random-forest.contextExtra5: Land cover classification from satellite imagery

naive-bayes.name: Naive Bayes
naive-bayes.summary: Classify using probabilities and Bayes' theorem
naive-bayes.contextDescription: Email providers use Naive Bayes to decide whether an incoming message is spam. By estimating how likely each word and feature is to appear in spam vs. legitimate mail, the model assigns a probability to each class and picks the most likely one.
naive-bayes.contextExtra1: Spam and phishing detection in email
naive-bayes.contextExtra3: Sentiment polarity detection on short texts

cnn.name: CNN
cnn.summary: Recognize patterns in images using neural networks
cnn.contextDescription: Hospitals use Convolutional Neural Networks to analyze chest X-rays and detect pneumonia. The CNN learns to identify patterns in medical images — from edges and textures to complex structures — that indicate the presence of disease.
cnn.contextExtra5: Satellite image analysis for urban planning

transformers.name: Transformers
transformers.summary: Understand text by learning word relationships
transformers.contextDescription: E-commerce platforms use Transformers to analyze product reviews and determine customer sentiment. The attention mechanism allows the model to understand context and nuance in text, distinguishing positive from negative opinions.
transformers.contextExtra5: Legal document analysis and contract review

kmeans.name: K-Means
kmeans.summary: Group data into clusters by similarity
kmeans.contextDescription: Retail companies use K-Means clustering to segment customers based on spending habits, visit frequency, and purchase values. By discovering natural groups in the data, businesses can tailor marketing strategies and promotions to each segment.
kmeans.contextExtra2: Document topic grouping for search engines
kmeans.contextExtra3: Anomaly detection in network traffic patterns
kmeans.contextExtra4: Geographic zone partitioning for delivery routes
kmeans.contextExtra5: Gene expression pattern discovery in genomics

gan.name: GAN
gan.summary: Generate data by adversarial competition between networks
gan.contextDescription: Creative studios and research labs use Generative Adversarial Networks to create synthetic data. A Generator network learns to produce realistic samples by competing against a Discriminator that tries to tell real from fake — the adversarial game drives both to improve until the generated data is indistinguishable from real.
gan.contextExtra1: Photorealistic image synthesis and style transfer
gan.contextExtra2: Data augmentation for training other ML models
gan.contextExtra3: Drug molecule design in pharmaceutical research
gan.contextExtra4: Video game asset and texture generation
gan.contextExtra5: Synthetic medical data for privacy-preserving research

rnn.name: RNN
rnn.summary: Predict sequences by learning temporal patterns
rnn.contextDescription: Weather stations use Recurrent Neural Networks to predict tomorrow's temperature based on historical patterns. The RNN processes a sequence of past temperatures, maintaining a hidden state that captures seasonal trends and short-term fluctuations.
rnn.contextExtra1: Stock price and financial time-series prediction
rnn.contextExtra5: Anomaly detection in sensor data streams
rnn.contextExtra6: Energy demand forecasting for power grids

metricsPage.title: Understanding Metrics
metricsPage.subtitle: Learn what each metric measures, the math behind it, and when to use it in real-world problems.
metricsPage.regressionDescription: Metrics for evaluating models that predict continuous numeric values.
metricsPage.classificationDescription: Metrics for evaluating models that predict discrete categories or classes.

mse.name: MSE — Mean Squared Error
mse.explanation: Measures the average of the squared differences between predicted and actual values. Larger errors are penalized more heavily due to squaring.
mse.interpretation: MSE is always ≥ 0. Lower is better. MSE = 0 means perfect predictions. The units are squared (e.g., dollars²), making it harder to interpret directly.
mse.whenToUse: Use MSE when large errors are particularly undesirable. For example, in energy consumption forecasting for power grids, a large prediction error could lead to blackouts or wasted energy — MSE ensures the model is heavily penalized for those dangerous outliers.
mse.whenNotToUse: Avoid MSE when you need an interpretable error in the same units as your target. For instance, if you're telling a client their house price estimate is off by '250,000 dollars²', that's meaningless. Use RMSE or MAE instead for human-readable reports.

rmse.name: RMSE — Root Mean Squared Error
rmse.explanation: The square root of MSE, bringing the error back to the same units as the target variable. Still penalizes large errors more than small ones.
rmse.interpretation: RMSE is always ≥ 0, in the same units as the prediction. Lower is better. An RMSE of 5,000 on house prices means 'on average, predictions are about $5,000 off'.
rmse.whenToUse: Use RMSE when you want an interpretable error metric that still penalizes outliers. For salary prediction in HR analytics, RMSE tells you the typical dollar amount your model is off by, while still flagging if it wildly misses some predictions.
rmse.whenNotToUse: Avoid RMSE when your data has many outliers that aren't your fault (e.g., noisy sensor data). A few extreme readings will inflate RMSE disproportionately, making a good model look bad. Use MAE for a more robust alternative.

mae.name: MAE — Mean Absolute Error
mae.explanation: Measures the average of the absolute differences between predicted and actual values. Treats all errors equally regardless of size.
mae.interpretation: MAE is always ≥ 0, in the same units as the prediction. Lower is better. MAE = 0 means perfect predictions. MAE is more robust to outliers than MSE/RMSE.
mae.whenToUse: Use MAE when all errors matter equally and outliers shouldn't dominate the evaluation. For delivery time estimation, whether you're off by 5 or 50 minutes matters proportionally — MAE gives you a fair average error without letting rare extreme delays skew the picture.
mae.whenNotToUse: Avoid MAE when large errors have disproportionate consequences. In predicting structural load capacity for bridges, a large error could be catastrophic — MAE would treat a 1-ton error and a 50-ton error too similarly. Use MSE/RMSE to heavily penalize those dangerous large errors.

r2.name: R² — Coefficient of Determination
r2.explanation: Measures the proportion of variance in the target variable that the model explains. Compares your model against a baseline that always predicts the mean.
r2.interpretation: R² ranges from -∞ to 1. R² = 1 means the model explains all variance (perfect). R² = 0 means the model is no better than predicting the mean. R² < 0 means the model is worse than the mean.
r2.whenToUse: Use R² to compare models on the same dataset. In real estate pricing, R² = 0.85 tells you the model explains 85% of price variation — an intuitive way to communicate model quality to non-technical stakeholders.
r2.whenNotToUse: Avoid relying on R² alone when your dataset is small or when comparing models across different datasets. R² always increases when you add more features (even irrelevant ones), which can be misleading. In financial forecasting with few data points, a high R² might indicate overfitting rather than genuine predictive power.

accuracy.name: Accuracy
accuracy.explanation: The proportion of all predictions that were correct. Simply: how often was the model right?
accuracy.interpretation: Accuracy ranges from 0 to 1 (or 0% to 100%). Higher is better. Accuracy = 1 means every prediction was correct.
accuracy.whenToUse: Use accuracy when classes are balanced (roughly equal number of examples per class). For sentiment analysis of product reviews where positive and negative reviews are equally common, accuracy gives a straightforward picture of model performance.
accuracy.whenNotToUse: Never use accuracy as the primary metric on imbalanced datasets. Imagine a rare disease screening where only 1% of patients are sick: a model that always predicts 'healthy' achieves 99% accuracy while missing every single sick patient. That 99% accuracy is useless — and dangerous. Use Precision, Recall, or F1 instead.

precision.name: Precision
precision.explanation: Of all the cases the model predicted as positive, how many were actually positive? Precision measures the quality of positive predictions.
precision.interpretation: Precision ranges from 0 to 1. Higher means fewer false positives. Precision = 1 means every positive prediction was correct (no false alarms).
precision.whenToUse: Use precision when false positives are costly. In email spam filtering, a false positive means a legitimate email goes to spam — the user misses an important message. High precision ensures that when the model says 'spam', it's almost certainly spam.
precision.whenNotToUse: Don't rely on precision alone when missing positives is dangerous. In emergency room triage, a model classifying patients for the red room (critical care): high precision means 'when we send someone to the red room, they really need it' — but it says nothing about patients who needed the red room but were sent home. A model that only flags the most obvious cases has great precision but lets critical patients die. You need Recall here.

recall.name: Recall (Sensitivity)
recall.explanation: Of all the actual positive cases, how many did the model correctly identify? Recall measures the model's ability to find all positives.
recall.interpretation: Recall ranges from 0 to 1. Higher means fewer false negatives. Recall = 1 means the model found every positive case (no misses).
recall.whenToUse: Use recall when missing a positive case has severe consequences. In cancer screening, a missed diagnosis (false negative) means a patient with cancer goes untreated. High recall ensures the model catches as many true cases as possible, even at the cost of some false alarms.
recall.whenNotToUse: Don't rely on recall alone when false positives have high costs. In fraud detection for a bank that freezes accounts on suspicion: maximizing recall catches all fraud, but if it also flags thousands of legitimate transactions, customers are locked out of their accounts. The bank faces angry customers and operational overload. Balance recall with precision using F1.

f1.name: F1-Score
f1.explanation: The harmonic mean of Precision and Recall. Provides a single score that balances both concerns — avoiding false positives and avoiding false negatives.
f1.interpretation: F1 ranges from 0 to 1. Higher is better. F1 = 1 means perfect precision AND recall. The harmonic mean ensures F1 is low if either precision or recall is low.
f1.whenToUse: Use F1 when you need to balance precision and recall. In content moderation for social media, you want to remove harmful content (high recall) without censoring legitimate posts (high precision). F1 gives a single number that reflects both concerns.
f1.whenNotToUse: Avoid F1 when precision and recall are not equally important for your problem. In airport security screening, recall is far more important than precision (missing a threat is catastrophic, extra bag checks are just inconvenient). F1 would unfairly penalize the system for having many false positives. Use recall with a precision threshold instead.

confusionMatrix.name: Confusion Matrix
confusionMatrix.explanation: A table showing the counts of True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP), and False Negatives (FN). The foundation from which Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1 are all derived.
confusionMatrix.interpretation: The diagonal (TP, TN) shows correct predictions. Off-diagonal (FP, FN) shows errors. Ideally, all values are on the diagonal. The matrix reveals WHERE the model fails, not just how often.
confusionMatrix.whenToUse: Always examine the confusion matrix when doing classification. In medical diagnosis with multiple conditions, the matrix reveals if the model consistently confuses pneumonia with bronchitis — information that a single metric like accuracy would hide.
confusionMatrix.whenNotToUse: The confusion matrix is not a single metric — you can't easily compare models using it alone. When you need a quick comparison between 10 models, use it alongside summary metrics (F1, AUC). The matrix is for diagnosis, not for ranking.

rocAuc.name: ROC Curve / AUC
rocAuc.explanation: The ROC (Receiver Operating Characteristic) curve plots the True Positive Rate (Recall) against the False Positive Rate at every possible classification threshold. AUC (Area Under the Curve) summarizes the entire ROC curve as a single number.
rocAuc.interpretation: AUC ranges from 0 to 1. AUC = 0.5 means the model is no better than random. AUC = 1.0 means perfect separation between classes. AUC > 0.9 is considered excellent, 0.7-0.9 is good, < 0.7 is poor.
rocAuc.whenToUse: Use AUC when you want to evaluate a model's discriminative ability independently of any specific threshold. In credit scoring, the bank will set its own risk threshold later — AUC tells you how well the model separates good from bad borrowers across all possible cutoff points.
rocAuc.whenNotToUse: Avoid AUC on highly imbalanced datasets. In rare fraud detection (0.01% fraud rate), the FPR denominator (true negatives) is enormous, so even thousands of false positives barely move FPR. The ROC curve looks great while the model floods the fraud team with false alerts. Use Precision-Recall curves instead.
